Candidate Evaluation Report: Elementary School Principal Position

Name Bio Personal Statement Panel Evaluation
Adrienne Kowalski Adrienne Kowalski grew up in Ann Arbor and studied elementary education at the University of Michigan, where she also completed a master's degree in educational leadership. She spent four years as a classroom teacher at Riverside Elementary School, then moved into the assistant principal role there, managing daily operations and supervising a staff of more than thirty. She has been serving as acting principal at Riverside since the retirement of the longtime principal three years ago. I came to school leadership because I believe the culture of a building begins with the adults in it, and that when teachers feel genuinely supported they bring their best selves to work every day. What I hope to bring to this role is high standards for everyone, visible support, and a willingness to have honest conversations when things are not working. I am particularly passionate about early literacy, because I have seen how quickly a child who struggles to read can disengage from school, and how powerful it is to reverse that trajectory with the right instruction at the right moment. The full panel was impressed by the clarity and groundedness of her professional vision. She spoke with equal conviction about curriculum, culture, and operations. Her salary expectation is slightly above the posted band, but the panel believes this is worth exploring given her depth of experience and recommends advancing her to the final round.
Marcus Delgado Marcus Delgado studied child development at the University of Texas at Austin and spent his early career as a classroom teacher at Cedarwood Elementary School, where he was selected twice to lead professional development workshops on differentiated instruction. He later served as curriculum coordinator at the same school before moving on to work as an instructional coach at a neighboring school, where he provides regular coaching cycles and leads collaborative planning sessions for teachers across all grade levels. The panel found him to be a thoughtful and self-aware candidate whose coaching background is evident in how he listens and responds. He asks clarifying questions before answering, which several members noted as a positive sign for how he might handle difficult conversations with staff or families. The panel has no significant reservations and recommends him for the final round.
Priya Nambiar I have always been drawn to the intersection of research and practice, which is why I chose to remain in my doctoral program even while holding a demanding leadership role. I believe that school leaders who read and think rigorously make better decisions than those who rely entirely on intuition, because intuition alone can lead us to replicate what has always been done without questioning it. I find the discipline of academic research energizing rather than distracting, and I believe it makes me a more deliberate and curious leader. She was referred by the current assistant principal, Tomas Ferreira, who spoke highly of her judgment and her ability to build trust across a diverse staff. The panel's only concern involves scheduling: she was candid about needing protected time on several evenings each week for her dissertation work. The recommendation is to have a follow-up conversation focused specifically on that question before deciding whether to advance her candidacy.
Jordan Whitfield Jordan Whitfield has spent her entire career at two schools, moving steadily from the classroom into senior leadership roles at each. She began as a primary grade teacher before becoming dean of students, where she managed attendance, family communication, and behavioral support for a population of roughly four hundred and fifty children. She subsequently moved to a neighboring school as assistant principal, a role she has held for several years. She does not hold a graduate degree, though she has completed substantial coursework in school administration at two institutions. I want to be honest about the fact that I never finished my graduate degree, because I think trying to obscure it would be the wrong way to start this conversation. What I can tell you is that I have been doing the work of school leadership for a long time, that I have made real mistakes and learned from them, and that the people I have worked alongside have consistently told me they felt heard and supported. I try to build organizations where every adult believes that every child is genuinely worth their full attention, and I would very much like the opportunity to do that here.
Svetlana Obrekov Svetlana Obrekov was educated in Bulgaria before coming to the United States to complete a master's degree in educational administration at Pennsylvania State University. She spent the early part of her career teaching the primary grades and introducing a structured approach to small-group reading instruction that was eventually adopted across her entire building. She later became assistant principal at the same school for five years, then held a senior instructional leader role at a neighboring school that was eliminated in a district budget reduction. She has been seeking a permanent principal position since then. The panel found her to be a poised and analytically strong candidate whose background in early literacy instruction is genuinely impressive. When the panel asked directly about her thinner experience in the upper elementary grades, she was honest and convincing, acknowledging the gap without overstating her familiarity. The panel considers her a strong candidate, recommends her for the final round, and notes that she is available to begin immediately.
Darnell Osei I am applying because I believe I am ready to lead, and because I have spent the last several years watching the principals I have worked under and thinking carefully about what separates a school where people are getting through the day from one where everyone feels genuinely invested in something larger than themselves. The urgency I feel about improving outcomes for the children in my community is a real asset, not something to apologize for, and I am committed to earning the trust of staff, families, and students one honest interaction at a time. The panel was genuinely impressed by his presence and the maturity of his thinking given his limited years of experience. His personal statement was unusually perceptive about the relational dimensions of school leadership, and several members remarked on it. The panel's one reservation is that he has not yet held a formal administrative role, and the move from lead teacher to principal is a significant one. The recommendation is to advance him to the final round while being transparent with him about that concern.
Camille Tran Camille Tran studied psychology as an undergraduate before completing a graduate degree in school counseling, and she spent the first eight years of her professional life as a school counselor at an elementary school before transitioning into the assistant principal role there. Her counseling background is unusual in this pool and has visibly shaped how she thinks about student support and the emotional climate of a building. She has supplemented her training with additional coursework in building administration through two summer institutes and is currently pursuing formal administrative licensure. My path into school leadership came through the counseling office rather than the classroom, and I think that shapes how I see the job in ways I consider genuine strengths. I spent years sitting across the table from children who were struggling and from families who were frightened, and I learned that almost every problem that looks like a behavior problem is also a relationship problem. What I want to bring to this role is a way of leading that keeps that truth visible at every level of the organization, from how we talk about students in our team meetings to how we greet families at the door.
Reuben Glasscock His application was incomplete at the submission deadline. The packet included only a brief cover letter, with no resume, letters of reference, or administrative credential documentation attached. He has been retained in the system per human resources policy but is not recommended for an interview at this time.
Fatima Al-Rashidi Fatima Al-Rashidi was born and educated in Lebanon before completing graduate studies in educational leadership in Germany, and she has since built her career in the United States. She began as a kindergarten teacher at an international school, working in a multilingual classroom and developing family communication materials in three languages. She has been working as an assistant principal for several years, managing building operations, leading new staff orientation, and collaborating closely with the school social worker and family liaison on support plans for students in difficulty. The panel believes her background in multilingual school communities is a particularly good fit for the demographics of this building, and her thinking about family engagement is the most fully developed of any candidate in this pool. The one area the panel wants to explore further is her experience presenting to large adult groups, since the documentation of that work is thinner than the rest of her materials. The panel recommends advancing her to the final round but structuring the visit to include a prepared presentation so that question can be fully answered.
Oliver Hutchins Oliver Hutchins was educated and spent his entire career in England before beginning the process of relocating to the United States. He completed a bachelor's degree in primary education and a postgraduate certificate in educational leadership, and has spent nearly two decades working in London primary schools. He moved from classroom teaching into a deputy headteacher role, which he held for eight years, and has since served as headteacher of a primary school. His work authorization in the United States is in progress and is expected to be resolved within two to three months. I want to explain why I am making this move, because I think it is the most important thing to understand about my application. I have spent my career in London schools serving communities with high levels of economic hardship, and I have found that work enormously meaningful. What I would bring from my years of headship is a settled professional identity, a clear theory of how schools improve over time, and a genuine love for the slow, relational work of building a place where children and adults both feel they belong. The panel considers him the most experienced candidate in the pool by a meaningful margin, and his interview was composed and consistently grounded in examples from his own practice. The panel's only hesitation is the work authorization timeline, which introduces real uncertainty about when he could start. The recommendation is to advance him to the final round, have a direct conversation about timing, and then decide whether to hold the position based on the superintendent's assessment of the school's needs.
